summary: Resolved the remaining workbook-design questions into a two-layer scoring model and performed a targeted official-doc check on Evidently Cloud/SaaS status. Added an analysis note recommending one row per OSS-plus-commercial tool family, explicit Unknown/Enterprise-only states, and an Evidently SaaS caveat because current official docs conflict.
next_angle: Move to synthesis: consolidate evidence into the public example package structure and generate the first workbook draft with explicit scoring, source, methodology, and caveat sheets.
open_questions: Synthesis still needs to translate the scoring weights and caveats into concrete workbook rows and formulas.
The final XLSX must still be generated and validated for Excel compatibility and non-overlapping table/filter ranges.

sources.title: Evidently Cloud v2 - Documentation
sources.note: Official FAQ says Evidently Cloud v2 launched in April 2025 and that new users are automatically enrolled, which conflicts with other current docs saying Evidently Cloud is no longer available as SaaS.

sources.title: Self-hosting - Evidently Documentation
sources.note: Official setup page repeats that Evidently Cloud is no longer available as SaaS and describes self-hosted platform/UI options.

findings.text: The workbook should represent OSS libraries with optional commercial platforms as one row per tool family by default, using split deployment/governance notes rather than separate rows. This fits DeepEval/Confident AI, Giskard OSS/Giskard Hub, and Evidently OSS/Platform unless the hosted product has materially different evaluator coverage.

Use two visible scoring layers: Evaluation Fit Score for RAG evaluation capability and Operational Fit Score for deployment, governance, and production readiness. Suggested weights: RAG metrics 20%, CI/regression 15%, dataset/testset workflow 15%, observability/tracing 15%, integration fit 10%, deployment/data control 10%, access control/retention/audit 10%, public evidence maturity 5%.

Evidently managed SaaS availability should be marked as conflicting public evidence / vendor confirmation required. Official docs simultaneously say Evidently Cloud is no longer available as SaaS, describe Cloud as a recommended hosted managed option, and say new users are enrolled in Cloud v2.

Add explicit workbook value states such as Strong, Moderate, Limited, Framework-native, Enterprise-only, Unknown/public evidence not found, and Deprecated/legacy so caveats are visible instead of hidden inside a blended score.

analysis_artifacts.note: Analysis bridge capturing workbook row strategy, scoring weights, explicit value states, and Evidently SaaS caveat for synthesis.

adequacy.goal_alignment: Not evaluated in this search iteration; the evidence now appears ready for synthesis rather than more discovery.
adequacy.coverage_summary: This iteration covered the remaining row-model, scoring-weight, and Evidently SaaS ambiguity questions. It did not generate or validate the final package or workbook.

covered_requirements.requirement: Use current public sources and prefer official documentation.
covered_requirements.evidence: Targeted Evidently check used official documentation pages only.

covered_requirements.requirement: Shape work toward a compact XLSX decision matrix.
covered_requirements.evidence: Added workbook row strategy, scoring weights, value states, and caveat handling.

coverage_gaps.gap: Public example package and XLSX workbook have not yet been generated.
Excel compatibility and non-overlapping table/filter ranges remain unvalidated.

evidence_risks.risk: Evidently Cloud/SaaS status is internally contradictory across current official docs.

contradictions.contradiction: Evidently docs say Cloud is no longer available as SaaS while Cloud v2 docs and the same FAQ still describe current/new-user Cloud availability.

adequacy.recommended_next_angle: Draft the public-safe README, final-report, sources, and workbook structure from accumulated findings; then move to verify/finalize after workbook validation.

validation_evidence.reason: All three open questions were answered or converted into an explicit workbook caveat.
Only two new official sources were added, keeping the research at the configured 30-source cap.
